title: 37-Key Polyphonic Synth
description: A from-scratch polyphonic synthesizer built around a salvaged keybed, discrete 555-based tone generation, and a signal chain full of things I wanted to try — fuzz, delay, transformer saturation, and a vibrato LFO. Designed and built by me as a learning project. It is not finished yet.
bullets: 37 polyphonic keys from a salvaged M-Audio Axiom keybed
12 × 555 oscillators covering one full octave, each divided by a CD4024 to produce 7 octaves
Square wave and filtered pseudo-sine outputs with blend knob
White noise generator on every key via a shared passive mix bus
LM386-based signal recovery and amplification throughout
Effects chain: fuzz (LM386, power sag), PT2399 delay, transformer saturation stage
555 monostable vibrato LFO on each oscillator
Output stage with passive compressor and ¼" jack

steps.title: Step 1 — Salvage the Keybed
steps.text: I pulled the keybed from a broken M-Audio Axiom 61. The MIDI calibration was shot and a few keys were cracked, but the keybed itself was usable. I cut out the section I needed with a Dremel — 37 keys, semi-weighted, decent feel.

steps.title: Step 2 — Modify the Membrane Switches
steps.text: I cut the PCB traces to separate the top and bottom contacts on each key. All the bottom contacts got tied together — that's the white noise bus, so noise plays on every key. For the note wires, I scraped the trace on each key, soldered a labeled wire (C1, C#1, D1...), and zip-tied the wiring harness to the board before soldering to keep things from pulling up. Where traces got damaged, I locked the wire down with super glue. Checked every connection with a multimeter before moving on.
steps.alt: Modified membrane switch PCB with individual key wires

steps.title: Step 3 — Signal Recovery Amp
steps.text: With all 37 wires summed through a passive 100k resistor mixer, the output was extremely quiet — over 100k resistance in the wiring alone. I was planning to use 100k resistors for the mix anyway, so the LM386 was a natural fit here for signal recovery. It runs on a single +9V supply, which is the only rail in this whole build.
steps.alt: LM386 amplifier circuit for signal recovery

steps.title: Step 4 — Tone Generator Board
steps.text: Twelve 555 timers, each tuned to one note of the chromatic scale. Each one feeds a CD4024 binary counter that divides the frequency down across 7 octaves — giving 84 available tones total. I select 37 of them to wire up the keyboard range I want. Tune 12 oscillators, get the whole thing in pitch.
steps.alt: 555-based tone generator board with CD4024 dividers

steps.title: Step 5 — The Mixer
steps.text: The mixer sums all the square wave outputs from the keyboard matrix and amplifies them. A copy of that summed signal also runs through four cascaded filters and gets amplified back up — this is the 'sine' output. It won't be a true sine when multiple notes are played, but a filtered version of the mix. Both outputs feed opposite sides of a blend knob. White noise and a line-in jack are on this board too. External controls: Oscillator Blend, Oscillator Volume, White Noise Volume, Line In Volume.
steps.alt: Mixer board with blend, volume, and noise controls

steps.title: Step 6 — Effects Chain
steps.text: Fuzz first: an LM386-based fuzz with a power sag feature that produces some deliberately unstable tones. Then a PT2399 delay — possibly two chips for longer times. After that, a transformer saturation stage where I'm driving a small AC transformer backwards to get harmonic saturation; controls for saturation bypass, input level, and output level. A 555 monostable LFO is connected to each oscillator for vibrato — that's the theory, anyway.
steps.alt: Effects chain: fuzz, PT2399 delay, transformer saturation

steps.title: Step 7 — Output Stage
steps.text: Final amplification with volume and tone controls, followed by a passive compressor, into a ¼" output jack. This stage is still being planned.
